title: Kourambiedes
subtitle: Greek almond shortbread buried in icing sugar
excerpt: Thirty Greek almond shortbread biscuits: a crumbly butter dough pressed with a whole clove, baked until just set, then smothered in icing sugar while still warm. The thick icing sugar coating is not decoration; it is the point.
sourceNotes: Kourambiedes are documented throughout Greek and broader Eastern Mediterranean cookery from at least the Byzantine period. Recipes appear in pre-1928 Greek household cookery texts and in Nikolaos Tselementes, Odigos Mageirikis (1910, public domain), which gives the almond shortbread form as a standard Greek celebration biscuit. Cross-referenced with pre-1928 Ottoman and Greek-diaspora cookery literature available through Greek national library archives.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin. They improve for 2 days as the icing sugar coating absorbs into the surface.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 170°C. Kourambiedes should be pale, not golden. Remove before they colour.
baking.decoratingTechnique: press one whole clove into each biscuit before baking; bury in icing sugar warm from the oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Kourambiedes are coated in icing sugar twice: once warm from the oven (when the sugar melts slightly into the surface) and once again when fully cold (to build up the thick white coating). The quantity of icing sugar is deliberately excessive: when the biscuit is bitten into, the icing sugar should dust everywhere and the ratio of crumble to sweetness should be almost alarming. Cutting back on the icing sugar produces a different biscuit.

content.text: Kourambiedes are Greek almond shortbread biscuits associated with Christmas and celebration. They are crumbly, rich with butter, and finished with the
that defines them: thick, white, and applied in layers while still warm. Thirty biscuits. They keep in a tin for a week and improve over the first two days.

items.prepNote: for the dough, plus 200 g for coating

content.text: Beat the very soft butter with the 60 g of icing sugar, vanilla, and egg yolks until pale and creamy, about 3 minutes. Add the brandy if using. Mix in the chopped almonds. Sift the flour and baking powder and fold in until a soft dough forms. Chill for 30 minutes; the dough should be firm enough to shape.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan (170°C conventional). Roll tablespoon-sized portions of dough into crescents or balls and place on lined baking trays, spaced 3 cm apart. Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until set and pale: they should not colour. Do not overbake.

Sift 200 g of icing sugar into a deep bowl or tray. While the biscuits are still warm from the oven, roll them gently in the icing sugar to coat thickly on all sides. Set on a rack. Once fully cold, roll again in the remaining icing sugar to build up the coating. Store in an airtight tin with any remaining icing sugar dusted over the top.
